1:
"And it came to pass, that after we were gotten from them, and had launched, we came with a straight course unto Coos, and the day following unto Rhodes, and from thence unto Patara:" The witnesses note the voyage from Miletus down the coast. The puritan Henry and the grammatical voices note the painful parting from the Ephesian elders. The word for "gotten from them" implies a wrenching separation (Robertson, on the tender Miletus farewell). Paul tore himself away and sailed on.

On the route, the witnesses note the stages: Coos (an island), Rhodes (famous for its colossus), and Patara (a port of Lycia). The ship sailed a straight course, island by island, down the coast toward Syria.

The witnesses note Luke's precise itinerary. The day-by-day record, with its named ports, displays the eyewitness narrative. At Patara Paul would find a ship bound for Phenicia (v.2). The verse continues the journey toward Jerusalem.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: after the painful parting from the Ephesian elders (the wrenching separation, Robertson), Paul sailed by Coos, Rhodes, and Patara — a straight course down the coast, port by port (the precise eyewitness itinerary). The verse displays the continuation of the journey toward Jerusalem: the ship coasting from Miletus to Patara. The verse marks the resumption of the voyage after the Miletus farewell: Paul, having torn himself away, presses on toward the holy city. The verse displays Luke's careful record of the route — the named islands and ports testifying to the eyewitness narrative. At Patara the journey would change ships (v.2), as Paul found a vessel bound directly for Phenicia. The verse displays the steady progress of the journey to Jerusalem, where bonds awaited (20:23). The day-by-day account, with its vivid detail, displays the reality of the voyage and the determination of the apostle pressing toward Jerusalem despite the dangers foretold. The verse opens the final stage of Paul's journey to Jerusalem — the voyage down the coast that would bring him, through Tyre and Caesarea, to the city where he would be arrested. The named ports mark the steady advance toward the appointed trial.
2:
"And finding a ship sailing over unto Phenicia, we went aboard, and set forth." The brief verse notes the change of ships at Patara. The grammatical voices note the arrangement. At Patara, a port of Lycia, Paul found a larger ship sailing directly across the sea to Phenicia (the coast of Syria), and embarked. This was a more direct route than coasting.

The witnesses note the providence in the swift passage. The ship bound for Phenicia would carry Paul across the open sea toward Tyre, hastening his journey to Jerusalem for Pentecost (20:16).

The verse marks a stage in the voyage: the change from the coasting vessel to the seagoing ship, setting a direct course for Phenicia. The journey toward Jerusalem continues apace.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: at Patara Paul found a ship sailing directly across to Phenicia and embarked — a more direct route than coasting, hastening the journey. The verse displays the change of ships: from the coasting vessel to the seagoing ship bound for Syria. The verse marks the swift passage toward Jerusalem: the direct course across the open sea to Phenicia, bringing Paul nearer the holy city. The verse displays the providence in the timely ship: a vessel bound for Phenicia, ready to carry Paul across the sea. The journey, pressing toward Pentecost (20:16), is advanced by this direct passage. The verse, brief as it is, displays the steady, providential progress of the voyage: the right ship found at the right time, setting a direct course for Syria. The change at Patara marks the transition to the final approach: across the sea to Tyre (v.3), then by Caesarea to Jerusalem. The verse displays Luke's precise record of the voyage and the determination of the apostle, finding the swiftest way to reach Jerusalem despite the bonds foretold. The ship bound for Phenicia carries Paul toward the appointed trial, the journey advancing toward its destined end at Jerusalem. The verse, though brief, advances the narrative with a single decisive step — the change of vessel that set Paul on a direct course for the Syrian coast and the trial awaiting him at Jerusalem. Luke records even this small turn of the journey, the providence visible in the readiness of a ship bound exactly where the apostle needed to go.
3:
"Now when we had discovered Cyprus, we left it on the left hand, and sailed into Syria, and landed at Tyre: for there the ship was to unlade her burden." The witnesses note the passage past Cyprus to Tyre. The grammatical voices note the nautical detail. "Discovered Cyprus" means came in sight of it; "left it on the left hand" means sailed south of it, toward Syria. The ship made for Tyre to unload its cargo.

On Tyre, the witnesses note the ancient Phoenician city. The ship was bound there to discharge its burden, and Paul would stay seven days with the disciples (v.4). Tyre, once the great trading city, now had a Christian church.

The witnesses note the providence in the stop. The week at Tyre, while the ship unloaded, allowed Paul to find and strengthen the disciples there (v.4) and to keep the Lord's Day with them (Ellicott). The verse marks the arrival in Syria.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the ship came in sight of Cyprus, passed south of it, and sailed to Tyre in Syria to unload its cargo — the precise nautical detail (Robertson). At Tyre, the ancient Phoenician city now having a church, Paul would stay a week with the disciples (v.4). The verse displays the arrival in Syria: the ship landing at Tyre, the journey nearing its end. The verse marks the providential stop: the week at Tyre, while the ship unloaded, allowing Paul to strengthen the disciples and keep the Lord's Day (Ellicott). The verse displays Luke's careful nautical record: Cyprus sighted and passed, the course set for Syria, the landing at Tyre. The verse displays the steady progress toward Jerusalem: the ship reaching the Syrian coast, Paul drawing near the holy city. The stop at Tyre, providential for the church there, also brought the first of the warnings about Jerusalem (v.4). The verse displays the journey's approach to its destined end: the landing in Syria, the week at Tyre, the gathering warnings of the bonds awaiting. The ship's arrival at Tyre marks the final stage of the sea voyage before the overland approach to Jerusalem.
4:
"And finding disciples, we tarried there seven days: who said to Paul through the Spirit, that he should not go up to Jerusalem." The witnesses note the disciples at Tyre warning Paul — the great crux of the Spirit forbidding what Paul (bound in the spirit, 20:22) pursued. On the difficulty, the witnesses largely agree the inspiration was of warning, not prohibition. Barnes: "It was not understood by Paul as a positive command... but as expressive of their earnest wish... an inspired prophetic warning that if he went, he went at the risk of his life." Clarke: "the Spirit foretold Paul's persecutions, but does not appear to have forbidden his journey." Robertson: "Paul interpreted the action of the Holy Spirit as information and warning although the disciples at Tyre gave it the form of a prohibition."

Ellicott explains the apparent clash: "men received the Spirit 'by measure,' and the prophets at Tyre, though foreseeing the danger, might yet be lacking in that higher inspiration which guided the decision of the Apostle." He boldly affirms Paul was right to go.

The Reformed Calvin gives the same: "there be diverse gifts of the Spirit; so that it is no marvel if those who excel in the gift of prophecy be sometimes destitute of judgment... The Lord showed to these brethren what should come to pass; yet they know not what is expedient." The Spirit revealed the danger; the disciples added the dissuasion from love.

The witnesses agree: the Tyre disciples, finding Paul, warned him "through the Spirit" not to go to Jerusalem — the great crux, since Paul went bound by the Spirit (20:22). They largely resolve it: the Spirit revealed the danger (the bonds awaiting), but the disciples, from love, added the dissuasion as their own inference (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, Calvin) — the prophecy true, but the application (don't go) their judgment, not the Spirit's command. Ellicott: the prophets foresaw the danger but lacked the higher inspiration guiding Paul; he was right to go. The verse displays the distinction between prophetic foresight and prudential counsel: the Spirit showed what would happen; the loving disciples drew the conclusion Paul should avoid it. The verse marks the first warning at Tyre, and the key principle: inspiration may reveal facts without dictating the response. Paul, knowing his calling, rightly persisted, while the disciples' warning displayed their love. The verse teaches that genuine spiritual gifts (prophecy) do not always carry infallible judgment about what is expedient, and that duty may call louder than warning (Robertson).
5:
"And when we had accomplished those days, we departed and went our way; and they all brought us on our way, with wives and children, till we were out of the city: and we kneeled down on the shore, and prayed." The witnesses note the affectionate farewell at Tyre. The grammatical voices note the whole church accompanying Paul. Robertson notes "the first mention of children in connection with the apostolic churches." The disciples, with wives and children, escorted Paul out of the city — a vivid, tender scene (an eyewitness touch).

On kneeling on the shore, the witnesses note the place of prayer. Barnes: "Any place may be proper for prayer... God is everywhere." Ellicott notes the beach became a proseuche (place of prayer). The shore, removed from the bustle, served for the parting prayer.

On the extempore prayer, Barnes argues against a set form: "scenes like this show that such a form was not needed... they offered an extempore prayer." The prayer was heartfelt, suited to the moment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: after the week at Tyre, the whole church — with wives and children (the first mention of children, Robertson) — escorted Paul out of the city, and they knelt on the shore and prayed (the beach a place of prayer, Ellicott; an extempore prayer, Barnes). The verse displays the tender farewell: the whole church, families and all, accompanying Paul, and the parting prayer on the shore. The verse marks the affectionate bond between Paul and the Tyre disciples: a week's acquaintance issuing in this loving send-off. The verse displays the beauty of Christian fellowship: the families escorting the apostle, kneeling together in prayer on the beach. The vivid scene — wives and children, the kneeling on the sand — displays the eyewitness narrative and the warmth of the early church. The verse displays the fitness of prayer at parting (as at Miletus, 20:36): commending one another to God. The shore prayer, heartfelt and extempore, displays the spontaneous devotion of the early Christians. The verse marks the loving farewell at Tyre, the church gathered on the shore to pray with the apostle before his departure toward Jerusalem and the bonds awaiting.
6:
"And when we had taken our leave one of another, we took ship; and they returned home again." The brief verse notes the parting at Tyre. The grammatical voices note the leave-taking (the kiss of peace, Clarke). The disciples and Paul bade each other farewell, Paul embarking and the disciples returning home.

On the scene, the witnesses note the public parting on the beach. Robertson: "This scene is in public as at Miletus, but they did not care." The farewell, though briefer than at Miletus, was affectionate.

The verse marks the resumption of the voyage: Paul aboard, the Tyre disciples returning to their homes. The journey toward Jerusalem continues.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: after the leave-taking (the kiss of peace, Clarke), Paul embarked and the disciples returned home — a public but affectionate parting on the beach (Robertson). The verse displays the close of the Tyre visit: the farewell, the embarking, the disciples going home. The verse marks the resumption of the voyage toward Jerusalem: Paul aboard, the week at Tyre ended. The verse displays the tender but brief parting: the disciples returning to their own places, Paul pressing on. The verse, brief as it is, completes the Tyre episode: the loving farewell, the prayer on the shore (v.5), the embarking. The verse displays the rhythm of the journey: arrival, fellowship, warning, prayer, and departure, repeated at each stop. The disciples returning home, and Paul embarking, mark the continuation of the journey toward the appointed trial at Jerusalem. The verse displays the warmth of the early church even in brief acquaintance: a week at Tyre producing this affectionate send-off. The parting at Tyre, with its prayer and farewell, sends Paul on toward Ptolemais (v.7) and Caesarea, and thence to Jerusalem. The verse marks the close of the Tyre visit and the resumption of the final approach to the holy city. The verse displays the unhurried tenderness of the early Christians, who would not let the apostle depart without prayer and embrace. Brief as the visit had been, the bond was real, and the parting on the beach — kneeling, praying, and bidding farewell — displays the warmth that knit the scattered churches together in a common love for the gospel and its messenger.
7:
"And when we had finished our course from Tyre, we came to Ptolemais, and saluted the brethren, and abode with them one day." The witnesses note the brief stop at Ptolemais. The grammatical voices note the city (the ancient Accho, modern Acre, Judges 1:31). Barnes and Robertson note it was the best harbor on the Palestinian coast, with a church already established (perhaps by the scattered believers, 11:19, or Philip).

On the one-day stay, the witnesses note the brevity. The ship put in for the night, and Paul greeted the brethren, staying only one day before continuing.

The Reformed Calvin notes Paul's diligence: even on this brief stop, he greeted and encouraged the brethren. The verse marks a stage in the journey toward Caesarea and Jerusalem.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the ship reached Ptolemais (the ancient Accho, modern Acre, the best harbor on the coast, Barnes, Robertson), where Paul greeted the brethren and stayed one day — the church there already established (perhaps by the scattered believers or Philip). The verse displays the brief stop at Ptolemais: the ship putting in, Paul greeting the brethren, the one-day stay. The verse marks a stage in the journey down the coast: Tyre, Ptolemais, then Caesarea (v.8). The verse displays Paul's diligence even on brief stops: greeting and encouraging the brethren wherever he came. The verse, brief as it is, continues the careful itinerary: the named ports, the established churches, the eyewitness record. The verse displays the spread of the gospel along the Phoenician and Palestinian coast: churches at Tyre and Ptolemais, founded by the scattered believers or by Philip. Paul's greeting of the brethren at each stop displays the fellowship of the churches and his pastoral care. The verse marks the approach to Caesarea, the next and longer stop, where Philip the evangelist and Agabus the prophet awaited (vv.8-11). The one-day stay at Ptolemais advances the journey toward Jerusalem, the apostle pressing on through the coastal churches toward the appointed trial. The verse displays, in its very brevity, the steady advance of the gospel along the coast and the apostle's unwearied fellowship with the brethren at every harbor. No stop was too short for a greeting, no church too small for a visit; Paul gathered and encouraged the believers at Ptolemais as he had at Tyre, pressing on toward Caesarea and Jerusalem.
8:
"And the next day we that were of Paul's company departed, and came unto Caesarea: and we entered into the house of Philip the evangelist, which was one of the seven; and abode with him." The witnesses note the arrival at Caesarea and the lodging with Philip. The puritan Henry notes Philip's history: "He was originally a deacon, one of the seven (6:5)... He was now and had long been an evangelist." Last seen at Caesarea after baptizing the eunuch (8:40), Philip now hosts Paul.

On "the evangelist," the witnesses note the office. Calvin: "the evangelists were in the midst between apostles and doctors. For it was a function next to the apostles to preach the gospel in all places." Philip, once a deacon, had risen to the evangelist's work.

On Caesarea, the witnesses note its significance: the place where the gospel first came to the Gentiles (Cornelius, ch. 10). Philip's house, large enough to host Paul's company, displays Christian hospitality (Henry, 1 Peter 4:9).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's company came to Caesarea and lodged with Philip the evangelist, one of the seven (6:5) — last seen at Caesarea after the eunuch's baptism (8:40), now risen from deacon to evangelist (the office between apostles and doctors, Calvin). Caesarea was where the gospel first reached the Gentiles (Cornelius, ch. 10); Philip's house, large enough to host the company, displayed Christian hospitality (Henry). The verse displays the providential lodging: Paul with Philip, the two great figures of the early mission. The verse marks the arrival at Caesarea, the longer stop before Jerusalem, where Agabus would prophesy (vv.10-11). The verse displays the development of Philip: from deacon (serving tables) to evangelist (preaching the gospel) — having used the office of deacon well, he purchased a good degree (Henry, 1 Timothy 3:13). The verse displays the hospitality of the early church: Philip welcoming Paul and his company. The lodging with Philip, the evangelist of Caesarea, marks a significant reunion of the laborers in the gospel. The verse sets the scene for the warnings at Caesarea (Philip's prophesying daughters, Agabus) before Paul's arrival at Jerusalem and the bonds awaiting.
9:
"And the same man had four daughters, virgins, which did prophesy." The witnesses note Philip's prophesying daughters — women exercising the gift of prophecy. On the gift, the grammatical voices note its reality. Barnes: "females sometimes partook of the prophetic influence, and foretold future events" (Acts 2:17). The fulfillment of Joel's prophecy that sons and daughters should prophesy (Henry).

On the nature of their prophesying, the witnesses debate its scope. Robertson notes the difficulty: Paul allows women to pray and prophesy with covered head (1 Corinthians 11:5) yet demands silence (1 Corinthians 14:34) — "not easy to reconcile." The Reformed Calvin resolves it: "forasmuch as he doth not suffer women to bear any public office in the Church, it is to be thought that they did prophesy at home, or in some private place, without the common assembly." Ellicott notes the "virgins" formed a class of women's service in the church.

On the significance, the witnesses note the honor to Philip's house (Calvin: "no small gift to have four daughters all endowed with the spirit of prophecy") and the marriage of an evangelist (against the Papist prohibition, Gill).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Philip's four virgin daughters prophesied — women exercising the gift (the fulfillment of Joel, Acts 2:17, Henry, Barnes). On the scope, they debate: Calvin holds they prophesied privately, at home, not in the public assembly (since women bear no public office, reconciling 1 Corinthians 11:5 with 14:34); Robertson notes the difficulty of reconciling Paul's allowance and restriction; Ellicott sees an order of consecrated "virgins." The verse displays the gift of prophecy in women: the daughters of Philip, honoring his house (Calvin). The verse marks the women prophets at Caesarea, a sign of the Spirit's outpouring on all flesh (Joel). The verse displays both the reality of the gift in women and the question of its public exercise: most hold (with Calvin) that such prophesying was private, not overthrowing the order of public teaching. The verse displays the honor of Philip's house — four daughters gifted with prophecy — and the marriage of an evangelist (against clerical celibacy, Gill). The prophesying daughters, like Agabus (v.10), exercised the prophetic gift; and their presence at Caesarea, where Paul lodged, displays the rich spiritual life of the early church.
10:
"And as we tarried there many days, there came down from Judaea a certain prophet, named Agabus." The witnesses note the arrival of Agabus the prophet. The grammatical voices note his identity. Robertson and Calvin identify him with the Agabus of 11:28, who foretold the famine. He came down from Judea to Caesarea, apparently to meet Paul.

On the purpose, the witnesses note the divine forewarning. Calvin: "the Lord meant every where to make known the bonds of his servant, partly that they might know that he entered the combat willingly, partly that they might perceive that he was appointed of God to be a champion." The prophecy was for the strengthening of others.

On the "many days," the witnesses note the longer stay at Caesarea (Ellicott: the voyage having been quicker than expected, there was time). Agabus came during this stay to deliver his prophecy (v.11).

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: during the longer stay at Caesarea, Agabus the prophet (the same who foretold the famine, 11:28, Robertson, Calvin) came down from Judea — apparently to meet Paul and deliver a prophecy. Calvin notes the purpose: to make known Paul's bonds, that the brethren might see he entered the combat willingly and was God's appointed champion. The verse displays the divine forewarning: the prophet coming to confirm what awaited Paul. The verse marks the approach of the great prophecy (v.11): Agabus's symbolic foretelling of Paul's binding. The verse displays the providence in the prophet's coming: God repeatedly warning Paul (and the brethren) of the bonds ahead, that none might think the apostle acted rashly or in ignorance. The verse displays the rich prophetic activity of the early church: Philip's daughters (v.9) and now Agabus, all exercising the gift. Agabus's coming, during the stay at Caesarea, sets the scene for his dramatic symbolic prophecy. The verse marks the final, fullest warning before Paul's arrival at Jerusalem: the prophet from Judea coming to foretell the binding awaiting the apostle. The verse displays the providence that brought prophet and apostle together at Caesarea, that the warning might be given with the fullest solemnity. Agabus, known already for the famine prophecy, comes now to enact the binding of Paul — God preparing both the apostle and the brethren for the trial, that none might think Paul rushed blindly or unwillingly into danger.
11:
"And when he was come unto us, he took Paul's girdle, and bound his own hands and feet, and said, Thus saith the Holy Ghost, So shall the Jews at Jerusalem bind the man that owneth this girdle, and shall deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles." The witnesses note Agabus's symbolic prophecy. On the symbolic act, the grammatical voices note the manner of the OT prophets. Barnes: "It was common for the prophets to perform actions which were emblematic of the events which they predicted" — Jeremiah's girdle and yokes (Jeremiah 13, 27), Isaiah walking naked (Isaiah 20), Ezekiel's signs. Agabus binding himself with Paul's girdle dramatized the coming binding.

On the prophecy, the witnesses note its precise fulfillment. Barnes and Clarke note "delivered into the hands of the Gentiles" — the Romans, who held the power of life and death. The prophecy was fulfilled at v.33 and following.

On the parallel with Christ, Robertson notes the words echo Jesus's prediction of his own delivery into Gentile hands (Matthew 20:19). Paul, like his Lord, would be bound and delivered to the Gentiles.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Agabus took Paul's girdle, bound his own hands and feet, and prophesied that the Jews would so bind Paul and deliver him to the Gentiles — a symbolic act in the manner of the OT prophets (Jeremiah, Isaiah, Ezekiel, Barnes, Clarke). The prophecy of delivery "into the hands of the Gentiles" (the Romans) was precisely fulfilled (v.33); and it echoed Jesus's own prediction (Matthew 20:19, Robertson) — Paul, like his Lord, bound and delivered to the Gentiles. The verse displays the dramatic, symbolic prophecy: Agabus binding himself to foretell Paul's binding. The verse marks the fullest warning of Paul's coming bonds, made vivid by the prophetic sign. The verse displays the continuity with the OT prophets: the symbolic act giving force to the prediction. The verse displays the precision of the prophecy: the Jews binding Paul, delivering him to the Gentiles — fulfilled in the riot and arrest (vv.27-33). The verse displays the parallel between Paul and Christ: both bound and delivered to the Gentiles, the servant sharing the Master's sufferings. Agabus's dramatic sign, "Thus saith the Holy Ghost," gave the clearest forewarning yet of the bonds awaiting Paul at Jerusalem, preparing both him and the brethren for the trial to come.
12:
"And when we heard these things, both we, and they of that place, besought him not to go up to Jerusalem." The witnesses note the renewed entreaty after Agabus's prophecy. The grammatical voices note that even Paul's companions now joined the dissuasion. Ellicott: "For the first time the courage even of the Apostle's companions began to fail, and St. Luke admits that he himself had joined in the entreaty." Robertson notes Luke and the company joined Philip and his daughters in "a chorus of dissuasion."

On the entreaty, the witnesses note the love behind it. The dramatic prophecy moved them to beg Paul not to go, fearing for his life. The Reformed Calvin notes their desire was worthy of praise — to provide for the common safety of the church by keeping Paul.

The witnesses note the understanding of the prophecy as conditional. Clarke: "they all understood the prophecy to be conditional and contingent." They thought Paul might avoid the danger by not going. But Paul understood the prophecy had the force of a command (Wesley).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: after Agabus's prophecy, all — including Paul's own companions (Luke admitting he joined, Ellicott) and the Caesarean disciples — begged Paul not to go to Jerusalem, fearing for his life. Their love prompted the entreaty (Calvin: worthy of praise, to keep Paul for the church's good). They understood the prophecy as conditional (Paul might avoid the danger, Clarke), though Paul took it as a command to go (Wesley). The verse displays the renewed, intensified dissuasion: even the courage of Paul's companions failing before the vivid prophecy. The verse marks the climax of the warnings: all joining to beg Paul not to go. The verse displays the tension between love and calling: the disciples' affectionate dissuasion against Paul's resolute obedience. The entreaty, far from a fault, displayed their love; but it could not turn Paul from his Spirit-led purpose. The verse displays the human reality of the parting: the chorus of dissuasion, the tears (v.13), the deep love for the apostle. Yet Paul, knowing his calling, would not be turned. The verse sets up Paul's noble resolve (v.13): undeterred by the warnings, ready to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord. The disciples' loving entreaty, though it could not move him, displays the affection that surrounded the apostle on his way to the appointed trial.
13:
"Then Paul answered, What mean ye to weep and to break mine heart? for I am ready not to be bound only, but also to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus." The witnesses note Paul's noble, tender resolve. On "weep and break mine heart," the witnesses note Paul's sensitivity. Ellicott: "The intense sensitiveness of St. Paul's nature shows itself in every syllable. It was with no Stoic hardness that he resisted their entreaties. They were positively crushing to him." Robertson: their weeping was "weakening Paul's determination." Yet he held firm, though with a broken heart.

On "ready not to be bound only, but also to die," the witnesses note the martyr spirit. Barnes: "This was the true spirit of a martyr." Robertson compares Paul's readiness to Jesus setting his face to Jerusalem (Luke 9:51). Paul was prepared not only for bonds but for death.

On "for the name of the Lord Jesus," the witnesses note the motive. Paul's readiness to die was for Christ's name — the noblest cause. His resolve rose to heroic proportions (Robertson).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, deeply moved ("what mean ye to weep and break my heart?" — his intense sensitivity, Ellicott; the weeping crushing to him, Robertson), yet firm, declared his readiness not only to be bound but to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus — the true martyr spirit (Barnes), like Jesus setting his face to Jerusalem (Luke 9:51, Robertson). The verse displays Paul's tender yet resolute heart: wounded by their tears, yet undeterred in his calling. The verse marks the height of Paul's devotion: ready to die for Christ's name. The verse displays both the sensitivity and the courage of the apostle: not a Stoic hardness but a broken heart, yet an unshaken resolve. Paul's love for the disciples made their tears painful; but his love for Christ made him ready to die. The verse displays the heroic stature of the apostle: prepared for bonds and death, for the name of the Lord Jesus. The verse displays the noblest motive: not stubbornness but devotion to Christ, for whose name Paul would gladly suffer and die. His tender yet firm answer — wounded by their love, yet ready to die — displays the martyr's spirit, undeterred by danger or the tears of friends, resolved to follow his calling to the end.
14:
"And when he would not be persuaded, we ceased, saying, The will of the Lord be done." The witnesses note the disciples' submission to God's will. On "would not be persuaded," the grammatical voices note Paul's unbroken resolve. Robertson: "Paul's will was not broken, not even bent." Finding him immovable, they ceased their entreaty.

On "the will of the Lord be done," the witnesses note the submission. Barnes: "They were now assured that it was the will of God that he should go. And they were now ready to submit to that will." The Reformed Calvin: "they yield lest they resist the Holy Spirit, whereby they understand that Paul is governed." Recognizing Paul's Spirit-led purpose, they submitted.

On the words, Ellicott notes they may echo the Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:10) and Christ's own submission in Gethsemane (Luke 22:42). The disciples bow to God's will, as the Lord had taught.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: finding Paul immovable (his will not even bent, Robertson), the disciples ceased entreating and submitted: "the will of the Lord be done" — recognizing Paul was governed by the Spirit (Calvin), they yielded to God's will (Barnes), in words echoing the Lord's Prayer and Gethsemane (Matthew 6:10; Luke 22:42, Ellicott). The verse displays the submission of love: the disciples, unable to dissuade Paul, bowing to God's will. The verse marks the resolution of the tension: not Paul yielding to their fears, but they yielding to God's purpose in him. The verse displays the model of Christian submission: when God's will is clear, even painful, to say "the will of the Lord be done." The disciples' acquiescence, after their failed entreaty (Robertson's "quaint naivete"), displays their faith: recognizing the Spirit's leading in Paul, they submit. The verse displays the bridle of all our affections: nothing so bitter that God's will may not mitigate it (Calvin). The verse marks the close of the Caesarea warnings: the disciples submitting, Paul resolved, all bowing to God's will. The submission "the will of the Lord be done" — echoing Christ's own — displays the faith that surrenders to God even in the face of suffering and loss. Paul presses on, the disciples submit, and all is committed to the Lord's will.
15:
"And after those days we took up our carriages, and went up to Jerusalem." The witnesses note the final stage of the journey. The grammatical voices note "carriages" means baggage (the things carried). The puritan Henry and others note Paul, having settled the matter of going, took up his baggage and set out for Jerusalem.

On "went up to Jerusalem," the witnesses note the customary idiom (going up to the holy city, set on a hill). Paul, with his company and the collection, made the overland journey from Caesarea to Jerusalem.

The witnesses note the resolution of the apostle. Having faced down the warnings (vv.4-14), Paul now resolutely sets out for Jerusalem, carrying the collection for the poor saints. The verse marks the arrival at the destination.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: after the days at Caesarea, Paul took up his baggage ("carriages," the things carried) and went up to Jerusalem (the customary idiom for the holy city) — resolutely setting out despite the warnings. The verse displays the final stage of the journey: the overland trip from Caesarea to Jerusalem. The verse marks the arrival at the destination of the long journey: Paul, with his company and the collection, reaching the holy city. The verse displays the apostle's resolution: having faced down all the dissuasions, he presses on to Jerusalem. The verse, brief as it is, completes the journey begun at Miletus (and ultimately at Corinth): Paul reaching Jerusalem, carrying the gift of the Gentile churches for the poor saints. The verse displays the determination of the apostle: undeterred by the prophecies of bonds, he goes up to Jerusalem to fulfill his purpose. The going up to Jerusalem marks the culmination of the journey and the approach of the trial foretold by Agabus (v.11). The verse displays Paul's steadfast obedience: pressing on to the city where bonds and afflictions awaited, to present the collection and bear witness for Christ. The journey's end at Jerusalem sets the scene for the meeting with James (v.18) and the events leading to Paul's arrest. The verse displays the quiet resolve behind the simple act of taking up the baggage: having heard every warning and refused every entreaty, Paul now sets his face toward Jerusalem as his Lord had done. The journey that began far away in Greece reaches its goal, the apostle carrying the Gentiles' gift and his own life toward the city of his appointed trial.
16:
"There went with us also certain of the disciples of Caesarea, and brought with them one Mnason of Cyprus, an old disciple, with whom we should lodge." The witnesses note the disciples accompanying Paul and the host Mnason. The grammatical voices note Mnason was "an old disciple" — a long-standing believer, perhaps converted early (from Cyprus, like Barnabas). He would host Paul's company at Jerusalem.

On the Caesarean disciples, the witnesses note their care. They accompanied Paul to Jerusalem and arranged lodging with Mnason — a provision for the company in the city, perhaps where Paul could stay safely among friends.

The witnesses note the providence in the arrangement. Mnason, an old disciple of Cyprus, opened his house to Paul — Christian hospitality (as Philip's, v.8). The verse marks the provision for Paul's stay at Jerusalem.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: certain Caesarean disciples accompanied Paul and brought Mnason of Cyprus, an old (long-standing) disciple, with whom the company would lodge — Christian hospitality and a safe lodging among friends. The verse displays the care of the Caesarean disciples: escorting Paul and arranging his lodging. The verse marks the provision for the stay at Jerusalem: Mnason, the old disciple, opening his house. The verse displays the fellowship of the early church: the disciples accompanying Paul, the host Mnason welcoming the company. The "old disciple" Mnason, perhaps an early convert from Cyprus (like Barnabas), displays the continuity of the faith — a long-standing believer hosting the apostle. The verse displays the providence in the arrangement: a safe lodging prepared for Paul among friends, perhaps a Hellenist sympathetic to the Gentile mission. The verse, brief as it is, displays the love and provision surrounding Paul on his arrival at Jerusalem: the escort, the host, the welcome. The lodging with Mnason marks the practical arrangements for the stay in the city where Paul would meet James (v.18) and face the events leading to his arrest. The verse displays the hospitality and care of the brethren, providing for the apostle as he came to the city of his trial.
17:
"And when we were come to Jerusalem, the brethren received us gladly." The brief verse notes the glad reception at Jerusalem. The grammatical voices note the warm welcome. The brethren — the Christians at Jerusalem — received Paul and his company gladly, a token of fellowship and love.

On the reception, the Reformed Calvin notes its sincerity. The brethren rejoiced at Paul's coming and the report of God's work among the Gentiles. This was a private, warm welcome before the formal meeting with James (v.18).

The witnesses note this is distinct from the formal reception by James and the elders (v.18). The first welcome was by the brethren generally; the next day Paul would meet the leaders. The verse marks the arrival and warm reception.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: when Paul came to Jerusalem, the brethren received him gladly — a warm, sincere welcome (Calvin), distinct from the formal meeting with James the next day (v.18). The verse displays the glad reception: the Jerusalem Christians welcoming Paul and his company with love. The verse marks the arrival at the destination: Paul received gladly by the brethren. The verse displays the fellowship of the church: the warm welcome of the apostle, despite the tensions the Judaizers had stirred (v.21). The brethren's gladness displays their love for Paul and their joy at his coming. The verse, brief as it is, marks the safe arrival of the long journey: Paul at Jerusalem, received with gladness. The verse displays the contrast between the loving welcome of the brethren and the hostility soon to come from the unbelieving Jews (vv.27-30). For now, the apostle is received gladly, the journey's end reached in fellowship and joy. The glad reception sets the scene for the formal meeting with James and the elders (v.18), where the report of God's work among the Gentiles would be given, and the delicate question of Paul's standing among the zealous Jewish believers addressed. The verse marks the warm, fellowship-filled arrival of the apostle at Jerusalem. The verse displays the warmth that met Paul on his arrival, a fellowship that would soon be tested by the suspicions of the law-zealous believers. For now, the brethren's gladness displays the love that bound the church together, and the safe arrival of the long and perilous journey is marked by the joy of reunion before the formal meeting with James and the elders.
18:
"And the day following Paul went in with us unto James; and all the elders were present." The witnesses note the formal meeting with James and the elders. The grammatical voices note James's prominence. This is James the Lord's brother, head of the Jerusalem church (as at the Council, ch. 15). All the elders gathered for the meeting — no apostles mentioned, James presiding.

Chrysostom notes the formality and the gathering. Paul came with his company to report to James and the elders, the leaders of the Jerusalem church.

On the meeting, the witnesses note its purpose: to report God's work among the Gentiles (v.19) and to address the situation among the Jewish believers (vv.20-25). The verse marks the formal reception by the church's leadership.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the next day Paul went with his company to James (the Lord's brother, head of the Jerusalem church, as at the Council, ch. 15), and all the elders were present — a formal gathering of the church's leadership, no apostles mentioned, James presiding (Chrysostom). The verse displays the formal meeting: Paul reporting to James and the elders. The verse marks the official reception by the Jerusalem church's leadership: the apostle coming to give account of his Gentile mission. The verse displays the order of the Jerusalem church: James as head, the elders gathered, the formal meeting convened. The verse displays the respect Paul showed the Jerusalem leadership: coming to report and to consult. The meeting would address both the joyful news of the Gentile mission (v.19) and the delicate problem of Paul's standing among the zealous Jewish believers (vv.20-25). The verse displays the centrality of James at Jerusalem: presiding over the elders, receiving Paul's report. The formal meeting with James and the elders marks the official reception of the apostle and the collection at Jerusalem, and sets the scene for the report (v.19) and the counsel about the vow (vv.20-26). The verse displays the orderly governance of the Jerusalem church and Paul's deference to its leadership as he came to the city of his trial. The verse displays the dignity and order of the Jerusalem church receiving its most famous missionary: James in the chair, the elders gathered, the apostle come to give account. It marks the formal counterpart to the brethren's glad welcome (v.17) — the leadership convened to hear the report of the Gentile mission and to address the delicate question of Paul's standing among the zealous believers.
19:
"And when he had saluted them, he declared particularly what things God had wrought among the Gentiles by his ministry." The witnesses note Paul's report of God's work. On "declared particularly," the grammatical voices note the detailed account. Paul recounted, one by one, the things God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry — the conversions, the churches founded, the signs.

On "what things God had wrought," the witnesses note Paul ascribed all to God. The puritan Henry and Calvin note Paul gave glory to God, not himself: God wrought the work "by his ministry," Paul the instrument.

On the purpose, the witnesses note the report magnified God's grace among the Gentiles, and presented the collection as the fruit of the Gentile churches' love. The verse displays the humble, detailed report of the apostle.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, having saluted them, declared in detail what God had wrought among the Gentiles by his ministry — recounting the conversions and churches one by one, ascribing all to God (the work God's, Paul the instrument, Henry, Calvin). The verse displays the humble, detailed report: Paul magnifying God's grace among the Gentiles. The verse marks the joyful account: the apostle reporting the fruit of his Gentile mission to the Jerusalem leadership. The verse displays Paul's humility: ascribing the work to God, not boasting of his own labor. The detailed report ("particularly," one by one) displays the abundance of God's work and the care of the apostle to give full account. The verse displays the substance of the meeting: the report of the Gentile mission, the evidence of God's grace, perhaps with the presentation of the collection. The verse displays the unity of the church: Paul reporting to James and the elders, the Gentile mission's fruit laid before the Jerusalem leaders. The report, magnifying God's work among the Gentiles, would be received with joy (v.20), even as it raised the delicate question of Paul's standing among the zealous Jewish believers. The verse displays the apostle's faithful, humble account of his ministry, giving God the glory for all that had been accomplished among the nations.
20:
"And when they heard it, they glorified the Lord, and said unto him, Thou seest, brother, how many thousands of Jews there are which believe; and they are all zealous of the law:" The witnesses note the glad response and the report of the zealous Jewish believers. On "glorified the Lord," the witnesses note the joy. Barnes: "True piety will rejoice in the spread of the gospel... by whatever instrumentality." James and the elders rejoiced at God's work among the Gentiles.

On "how many thousands... zealous of the law," the witnesses note the large number of Jewish believers still observing the law. Ellicott: "zealots for the law... the term was an almost technical one for the most rigid class." Barnes explains why they still observed it: the law had been God-appointed, they were trained to it, and its abolition was being left to providence (the coming destruction of Jerusalem).

The witnesses note the tension this created. The zealous Jewish believers, hearing rumors that Paul taught apostasy from Moses (v.21), were troubled. Robertson notes the Judaizers had prejudiced them against Paul. The verse sets up the problem James addresses.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: James and the elders glorified God at the report (rejoicing in the gospel's spread, Barnes), then noted the many thousands of Jewish believers, all zealous for the law — still observing the Mosaic rites (God-appointed, they trained to them, their abolition left to providence, Barnes). The verse displays both the joy at the Gentile mission and the tension with the law-zealous Jewish believers. The verse marks the delicate situation: a large body of Jewish Christians, zealous for the law, prejudiced against Paul by the Judaizers (Robertson). The verse displays the complexity of the early church: Gentile believers free from the law, Jewish believers still observing it zealously. The verse displays the problem James must address: the rumor (v.21) that Paul taught Jews to forsake Moses had troubled these zealous believers. The verse displays the persistence of Jewish observance among the believers: not yet seeing the law's abolition, they clung to it, and the destruction of Jerusalem would settle the matter (Barnes). The verse sets the scene for the counsel about the vow (vv.23-24): a means to show Paul's respect for the law and allay the suspicion of the zealous Jewish believers. The verse displays the tension Paul faced at Jerusalem: not from enemies but from the zealous believers, misled about his teaching.
21:
"And they are informed of thee, that thou teachest all the Jews which are among the Gentiles to forsake Moses, saying that they ought not to circumcise their children, neither to walk after the customs." The witnesses note the false rumor against Paul. On the charge, the grammatical voices note it was "apostasy from Moses" (Robertson). The Judaizers had spread the report that Paul taught the Jews of the dispersion to abandon the law, circumcision, and the customs.

On the truth of the charge, the witnesses note it was a slander, a distortion. Clarke: "from any thing that appears in this book to the contrary, this information was incorrect: we do not find Paul preaching thus to the Jews." Barnes: Paul taught that the law was not necessary for justification and not binding on Gentiles, but did not tell Jews to forsake Moses. Ellicott notes the charge had a grain of truth (Paul taught "circumcision is nothing," Galatians 5:6), but the personal accusation was false.

The witnesses note Paul's actual position. Robertson: Paul himself observed the law (circumcised Timothy, took the vow), being "to the Jews as a Jew" (1 Corinthians 9:20). The rumor distorted his teaching of Christian liberty into a command to apostatize.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Judaizers had spread the false rumor that Paul taught the dispersion Jews to forsake Moses, circumcision, and the customs — "apostasy from Moses" (Robertson). The charge was a slander (Paul did not preach thus, Clarke): he taught the law was not necessary for justification and not binding on Gentiles, but did not tell Jews to forsake it (Barnes); Ellicott notes a grain of truth (Paul's "circumcision is nothing") but the personal accusation was false. Paul himself observed the law as a Jew (Robertson, 1 Corinthians 9:20). The verse displays the slander against Paul: his teaching of Christian liberty distorted into a command to apostatize. The verse marks the false charge that troubled the zealous Jewish believers. The verse displays the malice of the Judaizers: misrepresenting Paul's gospel of freedom as an attack on Moses. The verse displays the delicate problem: the rumor, though false, had prejudiced many believers against Paul. The false rumor, the work of the Judaizers, displays the opposition Paul faced even among believers, and the need to clear his name.
22:
"What is it therefore? the multitude must needs come together: for they will hear that thou art come." The witnesses note the inevitability of a gathering. The grammatical voices note "what is it therefore?" — what is to be done? Robertson: James and the elders, not believing the slander but knowing many did, seek a course of action.

On "the multitude must needs come together," the witnesses note the certainty. Barnes: "There will inevitably be a tumultuous assemblage." The news of Paul's arrival would spread, and the zealous believers would gather, eager to see how he acted.

The Reformed Calvin notes the propriety: it would be absurd for so famous an apostle to hide; his avoiding the people would increase suspicion. The elders, seeking to prevent offense, propose a plan (vv.23-24). The verse marks the need for action.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: James asks "what is to be done?" — knowing the multitude would inevitably gather, hearing Paul had come (Robertson, Barnes). The zealous believers, eager to see how Paul acted, would assemble; and his avoiding them would increase suspicion (Calvin). The verse displays the need for action: a plan to allay the suspicion of the law-zealous believers. The verse marks the practical problem: Paul's arrival could not be hidden, and the multitude's gathering must be addressed. The verse displays the wisdom of the elders: seeking to prevent offense, neither hiding Paul nor letting the suspicion fester. The verse displays the delicacy of the situation: the famous apostle, slandered before the zealous believers, must do something to clear his name. The verse sets up the proposed plan (vv.23-24): the vow and purification to show Paul's respect for the law. The verse displays the pastoral concern of James and the elders: to nourish concord, preventing the offense of the multitude. The question "what is it therefore?" displays their search for a wise course — a way for Paul to demonstrate, by a public act, that the slander was false. The verse marks the transition from the problem (vv.20-21) to the proposed solution (vv.23-24): the gathering being inevitable, a plan was needed to disarm the suspicion and preserve the peace of the church.
23:
"Do therefore this that we say to thee: We have four men which have a vow on them;" The witnesses note James's proposed plan involving four men under a vow. On "do this that we say," the grammatical voices note it was advice, not command. Gill: "this is said not as commanding, but as advising; and not to what was a duty... but as a point of prudence." James counsels a course of action.

On the four men and the vow, the witnesses note it was a Nazarite vow (Numbers 6). Barnes and Clarke note the men, members of the Jerusalem church, had taken the vow, and its completion involved shaving the head and offering sacrifices. Ellicott notes James himself lived as a Nazarite (Hegesippus).

The Reformed Calvin reflects on the elders' counsel: perhaps too indulgent toward the zealous, yet aimed at concord. He notes the four men's vow was "superstitious," reflecting the Jewish believers' lingering attachment to the law, which the elders tolerated to avoid greater harm.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: James advised (not commanded, a point of prudence, Gill) that Paul join four men of the Jerusalem church who had a Nazarite vow (Numbers 6) — its completion involving the shaving of the head and sacrifices (Barnes, Clarke); James himself lived as a Nazarite (Ellicott). Calvin reflects that the elders' counsel, perhaps too indulgent toward the zealous, aimed at concord, tolerating the believers' lingering attachment to the law to avoid greater harm. The verse displays the proposed plan: Paul to associate himself with the four men's vow. The verse marks the wise (or, to some, over-indulgent) counsel of James: a public act of law-observance to clear Paul's name. The verse displays the delicate balance: accommodating the zealous Jewish believers without compromising the gospel. The verse displays the lingering attachment to the law among the Jerusalem believers, and the elders' pastoral toleration of it (Calvin). 24:
"Them take, and purify thyself with them, and be at charges with them, that they may shave their heads: and all may know that those things, whereof they were informed concerning thee, are nothing; but that thou thyself also walkest orderly, and keepest the law." The witnesses note the heart of the plan — and the great accommodation crux. On the act, the grammatical voices note Paul was to join the vow, purify himself, and pay the expenses (sacrifices), so the men could complete their vow by shaving their heads. Barnes and Robertson note this was a recognized act of piety, often paid for by wealthier believers (Agrippa did so).

On the purpose ("all may know... thou keepest the law"), the witnesses debate whether Paul should have done it. The Reformed Calvin defends it: "Paul did not dissemble, but sincerely professed that he did not hate the law... it was sufficient to remove the slander that he was a revolt from the law." Barnes and Clarke defend it as showing Paul "did not intend to undervalue the laws of Moses" — a matter of expediency, not principle.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley sharply questions it: "Ought he not... to have answered plainly, I do not keep the Mosaic law; neither need any of you? Yea, Peter doth not keep the law. And God himself expressly commanded him not to keep it." Wesley sees the counsel as flawed.

The witnesses agree: James advised Paul to join the four men's vow, purify himself, and pay for the sacrifices, so all might know the slander was false and that he kept the law. They divide on the rightness: Calvin defends it (no dissembling — Paul sincerely showed he did not hate the law, removing the slander); Barnes and Clarke defend it as a lawful matter of expediency (showing respect for Moses without compromising the gospel of grace, since the law was still tolerated). Wesley sharply questions it, holding Paul should have plainly declared the law abolished. The verse displays the accommodation crux: was Paul's act lawful condescension or compromising dissimulation? The verse marks the proposed demonstration: a public act of law-observance to clear Paul's name. The verse displays the tension in Paul's situation: clearing the slander without denying the gospel of liberty. Most witnesses defend Paul (the act lawful in things indifferent, to win the weak, 1 Corinthians 9:20); Wesley dissents, fearing compromise. The verse displays the delicate question of Christian accommodation to the law for the sake of the weak — lawful condescension to most, but questioned by Wesley as too great a deference.
25:
"As touching the Gentiles which believe, we have written and concluded that they observe no such thing, save only that they keep themselves from things offered to idols, and from blood, and from strangled, and from fornication." The witnesses note James's reference back to the apostolic decree. On the decree, the grammatical voices note James reaffirms the Council's decision (15:20, 29). Robertson: "James stands by that agreement and repeats the main items." The Gentiles are not bound to the law, only the four abstentions for concord.

On the purpose, the witnesses note James distinguishes Jew and Gentile. Barnes: the observance of the law was not required of Gentiles, but was a matter of expediency for Jewish believers. The advice to Paul concerned the Jewish believers; the Gentiles' freedom stood unchanged.

The Reformed Calvin notes the equality underlying the distinction: the same liberty belonged to both, but the Gentiles were named because the Jews clung to their observances. James upholds the Gentiles' freedom while addressing the Jewish believers' zeal. The verse guards the gospel of grace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: James reaffirms the apostolic decree (15:20, 29) — the Gentile believers bound to no such thing, only the four abstentions for concord (Robertson). The advice to Paul concerned the Jewish believers; the Gentiles' freedom stood unchanged (Barnes). Calvin notes the same liberty belonged to both, the Gentiles named because the Jews clung to their observances. The verse displays the safeguard of the gospel: the Gentiles' freedom from the law reaffirmed even as the Jewish believers' zeal is accommodated. The verse marks the careful distinction: Paul's accommodation (vv.24) was for the Jewish believers, not a re-imposition of the law on Gentiles. The verse displays James's consistency: standing by the Council's decision, guarding Gentile freedom while seeking peace with the zealous Jewish believers. The verse guards against misunderstanding: the vow Paul would take was not a denial of the Gentiles' liberty, which the decree secured. The verse displays the balance of the early church: Gentiles free from the law, Jewish believers accommodated in their zeal, the gospel of grace upheld. The reference to the decree reassures that Paul's accommodation did not compromise the Gentiles' freedom. The verse displays the wisdom of James: addressing the Jewish believers' concern without undoing the charter of Gentile liberty. 26:
"Then Paul took the men, and the next day purifying himself with them entered into the temple, to signify the accomplishment of the days of purification, until that an offering should be offered for every one of them." The witnesses note Paul's compliance — and the vindication or questioning of it. On Paul's act, the grammatical voices note he conformed to the letter of the advice. Robertson: "Paul was conforming to the letter." He joined the men, purified himself, entered the temple, and announced the completion of the vow.

On the rightness, the witnesses largely vindicate Paul. Barnes: "He violated none of the dictates of honesty and truth... he did no more here than he had done in a voluntary manner (18:18)." Robertson defends it against the charge of compromise: the act broke the power of the Judaizers' slander over the Jerusalem believers. Calvin defends it as condescension to win the weak, not dissembling.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley questions it: "Yielding his own judgment to their advice, which seemed to flow not out of spiritual but carnal wisdom; seeming to be what he really was not." Clarke notes the difficulty but defends it (vows referred to thanksgiving, not justification).

The witnesses agree: Paul took the men, purified himself, and entered the temple to announce the completion of the vow — conforming to the advice (Robertson). They largely vindicate him: he did no more than at Cenchrea (18:18), violating no truth (Barnes); the act broke the Judaizers' slander over the believers (Robertson); it was condescension to win the weak, not dissembling (Calvin). Wesley questions it (carnal wisdom, seeming what he was not); Clarke notes the difficulty but defends it. The verse displays Paul's accommodation: complying with James's counsel for the sake of peace. The verse marks the apostle's condescension to the weak: joining the vow to clear his name. The verse displays the debate over Paul's act: lawful accommodation (most) or compromise (Wesley). The verse displays the principle of "all things to all men" (1 Corinthians 9:20): Paul becoming as a Jew to win the Jews, in things indifferent. The verse marks Paul's willing submission to the elders' counsel, for the peace of the church — vindicated by most as lawful condescension, questioned by Wesley as too great a deference.
