27:
"And when the seven days were almost ended, the Jews which were of Asia, when they saw him in the temple, stirred up all the people, and laid hands on him," The witnesses note the outbreak of the riot. On the Asian Jews, the grammatical voices note they were Paul's enemies from Ephesus. Robertson: "Jews (not Jewish Christians) who hated him for his work there." They saw Paul in the temple and stirred up the crowd.

The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the irony: Paul, in the very act of conforming to the law to allay suspicion, was seized by those who falsely accused him of profaning the temple. The plan to disarm hostility became the occasion of the riot.

On "stirred up all the people," the witnesses note the inflaming of the crowd. The Asian Jews, unable to refute Paul, raised a tumult, laying hands on him. The verse marks the beginning of the assault that would lead to Paul's arrest.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: as the seven days neared their end, the Asian Jews (Paul's enemies from Ephesus, not Jewish Christians, Robertson) saw him in the temple, stirred up the crowd, and seized him. The irony is sharp (Henry): Paul, conforming to the law to allay suspicion, was seized by those falsely accusing him of profaning the temple — the plan to disarm hostility becoming the occasion of the riot. The verse displays the outbreak of the assault: the Asian Jews inflaming the crowd against Paul. The verse marks the beginning of the events Agabus foretold (v.11): the Jews laying hands on Paul. The verse displays the persistence of Paul's enemies: the Asian Jews, defeated at Ephesus, now attacking him at Jerusalem. The verse displays the providence amid the malice: the very accommodation meant to bring peace became the occasion of Paul's arrest — which would carry him, in bonds, to Rome (the fulfillment of his purpose, 19:21). The verse displays the irony of the situation: Paul seized while honoring the temple, falsely charged with defiling it. The riot, stirred by the Asian Jews, marks the turning-point: the apostle's seizure, the fulfillment of the prophecies of bonds, the beginning of the long imprisonment. The verse displays the malice of Paul's enemies and the providence that would use even their hatred to advance the gospel toward Rome.
28:
"Crying out, Men of Israel, help: This is the man, that teacheth all men every where against the people, and the law, and this place: and further brought Greeks also into the temple, and hath polluted this holy place." The witnesses note the false charges raised against Paul. On the charges, the grammatical voices note their resemblance to those against Stephen (6:13). Robertson: "Like the charges against Stephen and Jesus before him truth and falsehood are mixed." Paul was charged with teaching against the people, the law, and the temple, and with defiling the temple by bringing in a Gentile.

On the falsehood, the witnesses note the slander. Clarke: "This was a most deliberate and malicious untruth." Paul had brought no Greek into the temple; the charge was based on a false inference (v.29). The accusation of defiling the holy place was the most inflammatory, as bringing a Gentile beyond the court of the Gentiles was a capital offense.

The Reformed Calvin notes the furious hatred: they cry as if all religion were in danger, conceiving a false opinion and betraying their venom. The verse displays the inflammatory, false accusation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Asian Jews cried out false charges — that Paul taught against the people, the law, and the temple, and had defiled the temple by bringing in Greeks — charges resembling those against Stephen (6:13), mixing truth and falsehood (Robertson). The accusation was a malicious untruth (Clarke): Paul had brought no Greek into the temple, the charge based on a false inference (v.29); the defiling charge, the most inflammatory, since bringing a Gentile beyond the court was a capital offense. Calvin notes the furious hatred, crying as if all religion were in danger. The verse displays the inflammatory false accusation: Paul charged with the very crimes calculated to enrage the crowd. The verse marks the slander that sparked the riot: the false charge of defiling the temple. The verse displays the malice of Paul's enemies: distorting his teaching and inventing a temple-defilement to inflame the mob. The charges, like those against Stephen and Jesus, mixed distortion with falsehood, calculated to provoke a murderous response. The verse displays the danger Paul faced: a false accusation, in the temple, before a zealous crowd, of the gravest religious crime. The inflammatory charges — against the law, the people, the temple — display the desperate malice of the Asian Jews, unable to refute Paul, resorting to slander to destroy him. The verse marks the false accusation that would lead to the riot, Paul's beating, and his arrest by the Romans.
29:
"(For they had seen before with him in the city Trophimus an Ephesian, whom they supposed that Paul had brought into the temple.)" The witnesses note the false inference behind the charge. The grammatical voices note the basis: the Asian Jews had seen Trophimus (the Ephesian, 20:4) with Paul in the city, and inferred he had brought him into the temple. Robertson: "They did not see Trophimus with Paul now in the temple... They simply argued."

On the lesson, Barnes draws a striking application: "This is a most striking illustration of the manner in which accusations are often brought against others. They saw one thing, they inferred another... If men would state facts as they are, no small part of slanderous accusations would cease." The charge rested on supposition, not fact.

The witnesses note the recklessness of the mob. Robertson: "This is the way of the mob-mind in all ages. Many an innocent man has been rushed to his death by the fury of a lynching party." The false inference, presented as fact, inflamed the crowd.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge rested on a false inference — the Asian Jews had seen Trophimus the Ephesian (20:4) with Paul in the city and supposed he had brought him into the temple (Robertson) — though they had not seen Trophimus in the temple at all. Barnes draws the lesson: slanderous accusations often rest on inference, not fact; if men stated facts as they are, much slander would cease. Robertson notes the mob-mind, rushing the innocent to death on supposition. The verse displays the baseless foundation of the charge: a false inference presented as fact. The verse marks the injustice of the accusation: Paul condemned on supposition, not evidence. The verse displays the recklessness of the mob: inflamed by an inference, ready to kill an innocent man. The verse displays the nature of slander: seeing one thing (Trophimus with Paul in the city), inferring another (Paul bringing him into the temple), and charging it as fact. The verse displays the danger of unfounded accusation: the false inference, spread among the zealous crowd, sparked the riot. Barnes's lesson stands: much slander would cease if men stated only facts. The verse displays the providence amid the injustice: the false charge, though baseless, would lead to Paul's arrest and his journey to Rome. The reckless inference of the mob, condemning Paul on supposition, displays the malice and folly of his enemies and the injustice of the riot that followed.
30:
"And all the city was moved, and the people ran together: and they took Paul, and drew him out of the temple: and forthwith the doors were shut." The witnesses note the city in uproar and Paul dragged out. On "all the city was moved," the grammatical voices note the violent commotion. Robertson: the cry "spread like wildfire over the city and there was a pell-mell scramble." The whole city was thrown into tumult.

On dragging Paul out and shutting the doors, the witnesses note the design. Barnes: "Under the pretence that he had defiled it. The evident design was to put him to death." Clarke and Wesley note the doors were shut to prevent Paul taking sanctuary at the altar, and to kill him outside the holy place without defiling it with blood.

The Reformed Calvin notes the perverse zeal: the city moved against Paul before hearing his cause, condemning him unheard — the mark of mad rashness, not true zeal. The verse displays the murderous tumult.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the whole city was thrown into tumult (the cry spreading like wildfire, Robertson), and they dragged Paul out of the temple, the doors being shut — with the design to kill him (Barnes), the doors shut to prevent sanctuary and to kill him outside without defiling the holy place (Clarke, Wesley). Calvin notes the perverse zeal: condemning Paul unheard, the mark of mad rashness. The verse displays the murderous tumult: the city in uproar, Paul dragged out to be killed. The verse marks the violence of the riot: the mob seizing Paul, the doors shut, the intent to murder. The verse displays the fury of the crowd: inflamed by the false charge (v.28), rushing to kill the apostle. The verse displays the irony: Paul, honoring the temple, dragged from it to be slain. The shutting of the doors, to prevent sanctuary and avoid defiling the holy place with blood, displays the murderous intent cloaked in a perverse care for ceremonial purity. The verse displays the perverse zeal Calvin condemns: the mob condemning Paul unheard, mad rashness masquerading as devotion. The verse marks the climax of the assault: Paul dragged out, the doors shut, the crowd about to kill him — until the Roman intervention (v.31) preserved his life. The murderous tumult displays the hatred of Paul's enemies and the providence that would deliver him.
31:
"And as they went about to kill him, tidings came unto the chief captain of the band, that all Jerusalem was in an uproar." The witnesses note the Roman intervention. On "went about to kill him," the grammatical voices note the murderous intent. The mob was beating Paul, seeking to kill him, as they had Stephen — without trial or charge.

On the chief captain, the witnesses note the Roman commander. Barnes and Ellicott describe the cohort stationed in the Tower of Antonia, overlooking the temple, with Claudius Lysias the chiliarch (commander of a thousand). The Roman guard was on alert during the feast.

The Reformed Calvin notes the providence: "the wonderful goodness of God, when he raiseth up the chief captain at a sudden, that he may deliver Paul from death... the Lord showeth a more evident token of his providence, because Paul's life was delivered without man's counsel." The Roman, coming to quell the riot, unwittingly saved Paul.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: as the mob sought to kill Paul, word reached the chief captain (Claudius Lysias, the chiliarch commanding the cohort in the Tower of Antonia, overlooking the temple, Barnes, Ellicott) that all Jerusalem was in uproar. Calvin notes the providence: God raising up the captain to deliver Paul without man's counsel — the Roman, coming to quell the riot, saving the apostle. The verse displays the providential rescue: the Roman intervention as Paul was about to be killed. The verse marks the deliverance: the Roman guard, alerted to the tumult, coming to Paul's aid. The verse displays God's providence amid the danger: the apostle's life preserved by the unwitting agency of the Roman commander. The verse displays the role of the civil power: the Roman guard, stationed to keep order, intervening to save Paul from the mob — as Gallio had done at Corinth (18:14-17). The verse displays the fulfillment of Agabus's prophecy (v.11): Paul delivered into the hands of the Gentiles, though here for his preservation. The verse marks the turning-point of the riot: the Roman intervention, halting the murder, beginning the long custody that would carry Paul to Rome. The providence that raised up the chief captain at the crucial moment displays God's care for his servant, preserving his life for further witness and for the journey to Rome.
32:
"Who immediately took soldiers and centurions, and ran down unto them: and when they saw the chief captain and the soldiers, they left beating of Paul." The witnesses note the soldiers' arrival halting the beating. The grammatical voices note the swift response. Robertson: the chiliarch took several hundred soldiers (centurions with each hundred) and ran down from the tower. The plural "centurions" shows a large force.

On the mob stopping, the witnesses note the effect. The crowd, beating Paul, stopped at the sight of the soldiers — "before the job was over" (Robertson). The Roman presence overawed the mob.

The Reformed Calvin notes the contrast: those whom neither God's majesty nor the temple could restrain relent before a profane soldier — showing they were driven by barbarous cruelty, not zeal. The verse displays the rescue.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the chief captain swiftly took soldiers and centurions (a large force, Robertson) and ran down from the tower, and the mob stopped beating Paul at the sight of them — overawed by the Roman presence (before the job was over, Robertson). Calvin notes the contrast: those unrestrained by God's majesty or the temple relent before a soldier, showing they acted from cruelty, not zeal. The verse displays the rescue: the soldiers' arrival halting the beating. The verse marks the Roman deliverance: the show of force stopping the murder. The verse displays the providence in the timely intervention: the soldiers arriving as Paul was being beaten, the mob desisting. The verse displays the power of the civil authority to restrain violence: the Roman soldiers overawing the murderous crowd. Calvin's observation is telling: the mob, claiming zeal for the temple, stopped not from reverence but from fear of the soldiers — proof their zeal was barbarous cruelty. The verse displays God's deliverance through the Roman power: Paul snatched from death by the soldiers' arrival. The verse marks the rescue of the apostle, halting the riot and beginning the custody that would preserve his life. The swift Roman response, halting the beating, displays the providence that delivered Paul from the murderous mob, preserving him for the witness ahead and the journey to Rome.
33:
"Then the chief captain came near, and took him, and commanded him to be bound with two chains; and demanded who he was, and what he had done." The witnesses note Paul bound and questioned. On "bound with two chains," the witnesses note the fulfillment of Agabus's prophecy (v.11). Barnes and Clarke note Paul was bound between two soldiers, his arms chained to theirs (as 12:6). The two chains marked him as a dangerous prisoner.

On the binding, the Reformed Calvin notes the captain's relative equity: though he binds Paul as an evildoer, he is fairer than the Jews, intending to hear him before condemning. The binding fulfilled the prophecy, though by the Roman hand.

On the questioning, the witnesses note the captain's inquiry: who Paul was and what he had done. Robertson notes Lysias "repeated his inquiries," seeking to understand the cause of the riot. The verse displays the arrest and the beginning of the inquiry.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the chief captain took Paul and commanded him bound with two chains (fulfilling Agabus's prophecy, v.11) — bound between two soldiers (Barnes, Clarke, as 12:6) as a dangerous prisoner — and demanded who he was and what he had done. Calvin notes the captain's relative equity: binding Paul as an evildoer, yet fairer than the Jews, intending to hear him. The verse displays the arrest: Paul bound and questioned by the Roman commander. The verse marks the fulfillment of the prophecy: Paul bound, delivered into Gentile hands (v.11). The verse displays the providence in the arrest: the binding that fulfilled the prophecy also preserved Paul from the mob. The verse displays the contrast between Roman justice and Jewish fury: the captain, though binding Paul, would hear him, unlike the murderous crowd. The verse displays the beginning of the long custody: Paul bound, the inquiry begun, the journey to Rome set in motion. The two chains, marking Paul as a notorious prisoner (the captain suspecting him the Egyptian rebel, v.38), fulfilled Agabus's word exactly. The verse displays God's providence working through the Roman arrest: the binding foretold, the apostle preserved, the inquiry begun that would lead, through trials and appeals, to Paul's witness at Rome. The arrest marks the beginning of the bonds that would carry the gospel to Caesar's household.
34:
"And some cried one thing, some another, among the multitude: and when he could not know the certainty for the tumult, he commanded him to be carried into the castle." The witnesses note the confused clamor and Paul's removal to the castle. On the confusion, the grammatical voices note the parallel with Ephesus (19:32). Ellicott: "We note the parallelism with the like confused clamour at Ephesus." The mob shouted contradictory things, none coherent.

On the captain's inability to learn the truth, the witnesses note the chaos. Robertson: "when he could not know the certainty." Unable to discover the facts amid the uproar, the captain ordered Paul carried into the castle (the Tower of Antonia).

The Reformed Calvin notes the madness of the mob: contradictory cries, yet all desiring Paul's death though convicted of no offense — devilish madness, not true zeal. The verse displays the removal of Paul to safety.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the mob shouted contradictory things (as at Ephesus, 19:32, Ellicott), and the captain, unable to learn the truth amid the tumult (Robertson), ordered Paul carried into the castle (the Tower of Antonia). Calvin notes the madness: contradictory cries, yet all desiring Paul's death though he was convicted of nothing — devilish madness, not zeal. The verse displays the confused clamor and Paul's removal to the castle. The verse marks the captain's prudent action: removing Paul from the chaos to learn the truth. The verse displays the irrationality of the mob: shouting contradictions, united only in their hatred of Paul. The verse displays the providence in the removal: Paul carried into the castle, safe from the crowd. The verse displays the contrast between the disorder of the mob and the order of the Roman authority: the captain, unable to make sense of the clamor, removing Paul to the castle for orderly inquiry. The verse displays the madness Calvin condemns: a crowd seeking the death of an innocent man, unable even to agree on the charge. The removal to the castle marks the next stage of Paul's custody: brought to safety, the inquiry to continue. The verse displays God's providence in the Roman protection: Paul preserved from the murderous, confused mob, carried into the castle where he would gain a hearing (vv.37-40). The confused clamor displays the irrational hatred of Paul's enemies and the providence that delivered him.
35:
"And when he came upon the stairs, so it was, that he was borne of the soldiers for the violence of the people." The witnesses note Paul carried up the stairs by the soldiers. On the stairs, the grammatical voices note the flights leading from the temple area to the Tower of Antonia. Barnes and Ellicott describe the steep stairs, the violence of the crowd so great that the soldiers had to carry Paul.

On "borne of the soldiers," the witnesses note the literal lifting. Ellicott: "the soldiers had literally to lift him from his legs and carry him in, while the troops lined the staircase." The press of the mob was so fierce that Paul was carried bodily.

The witnesses note the danger and the deliverance. The crowd's violence threatened Paul even as the soldiers bore him away; yet the Roman guard preserved him. The verse displays the perilous removal.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: on the stairs leading to the castle, the violence of the crowd was so great that the soldiers had to carry Paul bodily (literally lifting him, the troops lining the staircase, Ellicott). The verse displays the perilous removal: Paul borne up the stairs amid the mob's fury. The verse marks the intensity of the danger: the crowd pressing so violently that Paul had to be carried. The verse displays the deliverance amid the peril: the soldiers bearing Paul away from the murderous mob. The verse displays the fierceness of the crowd's hatred: their violence so great that the apostle could not walk but was carried. The verse displays the providence in the Roman protection: the soldiers shielding Paul, bearing him up to safety despite the mob. The vivid detail — Paul carried up the stairs by the soldiers — displays the eyewitness narrative and the extremity of the danger. The verse marks the climax of the physical peril: Paul borne aloft by the soldiers, the crowd surging below. The deliverance, through the Roman guard, displays God's providence preserving the apostle from the violence of the people. The verse displays the reality of the riot's fury and the firmness of the Roman rescue: Paul carried to safety, the mob's violence overcome by the soldiers' strength. The borne-aloft apostle, shielded by the soldiers, displays both the hatred of his enemies and the providence that delivered him to the castle and a hearing.
36:
"For the multitude of the people followed after, crying, Away with him." The brief verse notes the mob's murderous cry. On "away with him," the witnesses note the demand for death. Barnes and Clarke: "to death... for so much this phrase always means in the mouth of a Jewish mob." Ellicott and Robertson note the very cry raised against Jesus at the crucifixion (Luke 23:18; John 19:15).

On the parallel with Christ, Robertson notes: "He will hear it again from this same crowd (22:22)... Paul was learning what it was to share the sufferings of Christ." The cry that condemned Jesus now pursued his apostle.

The witnesses note the persistence of the mob. Cheated of lynching Paul, they followed, still demanding his death. The verse displays the murderous cry pursuing the apostle.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the mob followed, crying "away with him" — the demand for death (the phrase always meaning death in a Jewish mob, Barnes, Clarke), the very cry raised against Jesus at the crucifixion (Luke 23:18; John 19:15, Ellicott, Robertson). Robertson notes Paul was sharing the sufferings of Christ, the same cry pursuing him. The verse displays the murderous cry: the mob, cheated of lynching Paul, still demanding his death. The verse marks the persistence of the hatred: the crowd following, crying for Paul's death. The verse displays the parallel between Paul and Christ: the same cry, "away with him," that condemned the Lord now pursued his apostle. The verse displays the fury of the mob: not content with the failed lynching, following to demand Paul's death from the Romans. The cry "away with him," echoing the crucifixion, displays the deep hatred of Paul's enemies and the apostle's fellowship in Christ's sufferings. The verse displays the relentless malice of the crowd, pursuing Paul even as the soldiers bore him away. The murderous cry, the same that doomed Jesus, marks the depth of the opposition Paul faced and his conformity to his suffering Lord. The verse displays the persistence of the mob's hatred and the providence that, through the Roman guard, preserved Paul from the death the crowd demanded. The cry "away with him" pursues the apostle as it had pursued his Master, displaying his share in the sufferings of Christ.
37:
"And as Paul was to be led into the castle, he said unto the chief captain, May I speak unto thee? Who said, Canst thou speak Greek?" The witnesses note Paul's request and the captain's surprise. On Paul's calm request, the grammatical voices note his composure. Robertson: "The calm self-control of Paul in the presence of this mob is amazing. His courteous request to Lysias was in Greek to the chiliarch's amazement." Amid the uproar, Paul calmly asks to speak.

On "canst thou speak Greek?", the witnesses note the captain's surprise. Ellicott: "The chiliarch apparently expected his prisoner to have spoken Hebrew, and was surprised to hear Greek." Paul's Greek showed he was no ignorant rabble-rouser.

The Reformed Calvin notes Paul's purpose: to defend his cause, as all God's servants must, making their integrity known lest God's name be blasphemed. The verse displays Paul's composure and courtesy.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: as Paul was led into the castle, he calmly and courteously asked the chief captain, "May I speak?" in Greek — his amazing self-control amid the mob (Robertson) — and the captain, surprised to hear Greek (expecting Hebrew, Ellicott), asked if he could speak it. Calvin notes Paul's purpose: to defend his cause and make his integrity known, as God's servants must. The verse displays Paul's composure: calm and courteous in the midst of the uproar. The verse marks the turn toward Paul's defense: his request to speak, his Greek surprising the captain. The verse displays the contrast between Paul's calm and the mob's fury: the apostle, bound and beaten, courteously requesting to speak. The verse displays Paul's readiness to defend the gospel: seizing the opportunity, even in chains, to bear witness. The captain's surprise at Paul's Greek displays the apostle's culture and standing, distinguishing him from the rabble. The verse displays the providence in the opportunity: Paul, bound and carried, gaining a hearing through his calm request and his Greek. The verse marks the beginning of Paul's defense (which would continue in ch. 22): the apostle, undaunted by the mob, courteously asking to speak, and the captain, intrigued by his Greek, granting a hearing. Paul's composure and courtesy, amid the danger, display the steadiness of the servant of Christ and his readiness to seize every opportunity for witness.
38:
"Art not thou that Egyptian, which before these days madest an uproar, and leddest out into the wilderness four thousand men that were murderers?" The witnesses note the captain's mistaken identification — and the historical reference to the Egyptian. On the Egyptian, the grammatical voices cite Josephus. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott note the Egyptian false prophet who, under Felix, led a multitude to the Mount of Olives, promising the walls would fall; Felix routed them, but the Egyptian escaped. The captain supposed Paul was this fugitive.

On the discrepancy (Luke's 4,000 vs Josephus's 30,000), the witnesses defend Luke. Barnes: "Why should Josephus be esteemed infallible, and Luke false?" He offers solutions: Luke may refer to the armed core (the Sicarii/assassins), or Josephus exaggerated, or there was a copyist's error (Δ four mistaken for Λ thirty), or the numbers refer to different stages. Clarke and others concur.

On "murderers" (Sicarii), the witnesses note the assassins who carried concealed daggers (sica). The captain took Paul for the leader of these banditti, explaining the two chains (v.33).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the captain mistook Paul for the Egyptian false prophet who, under Felix, had led a multitude to the Mount of Olives and escaped (Josephus, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott). On the number discrepancy (Luke's 4,000 vs Josephus's 30,000), the witnesses defend Luke — Josephus may exaggerate, or Luke refers to the armed core (the Sicarii), or a copyist confused Δ (four) with Λ (thirty), or the numbers reflect different stages (Barnes, Clarke). The "murderers" are the Sicarii, assassins with concealed daggers. The verse displays the captain's mistaken identification: taking Paul for the fugitive Egyptian rebel. The verse marks the historical anchor: the reference to a known event (the Egyptian's revolt). The verse displays the defense of Luke's accuracy against Josephus: the apparent discrepancy resolved by exaggeration, different reckonings, or a copyist's error. The verse displays the providence in the captain's question: clarifying Paul was no rebel, opening the way for his defense. The verse displays the historical reliability of Acts: the reference to the Egyptian, attested by Josephus, anchoring the narrative in known events. The captain's mistake — taking Paul for the assassin-leader — explains the two chains and sets up Paul's correction (v.39). The verse displays both the historical detail and the apologetic defense of Luke's precision, the apparent conflict with Josephus resolvable in Luke's favor.
39:
"But Paul said, I am a man which am a Jew of Tarsus, a city in Cilicia, a citizen of no mean city: and, I beseech thee, suffer me to speak unto the people." The witnesses note Paul's correction and request. On "a Jew of Tarsus," the grammatical voices note Paul's identity. Chrysostom: "he means his religion... he was both a Jew and a Christian." Paul declares his nationality and birthplace, distinguishing himself from the Egyptian.

On "no mean city," the witnesses note Tarsus's fame. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note Tarsus was a renowned center of learning, rival to Athens and Alexandria, called "no mean city" (litotes for a distinguished one). Paul's standing as a citizen of Tarsus answered the captain's suspicion.

On the request, the witnesses note Paul's boldness. Robertson: "a strange request and a daring one, to wish to speak to this mob howling for Paul's blood." Paul, undaunted, asks to address the very crowd that sought his death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul corrected the captain — "a Jew of Tarsus... a citizen of no mean city" — declaring his nationality and his standing as a citizen of the renowned Tarsus (rival to Athens and Alexandria, Barnes, Robertson), and boldly requested to address the people. Chrysostom notes Paul declares his religion (a Jew, and also a Christian); the request to speak to the murderous mob was strange and daring (Robertson). The verse displays Paul's correction and bold request: identifying himself, then asking to address the crowd. The verse marks the apostle's courage: undaunted, seeking to speak to the very mob that sought his death. The verse displays Paul's standing: a Jew, a citizen of a famous city, no rebel — answering the captain's suspicion. The verse displays the boldness of the apostle: turning the danger into an opportunity for witness, requesting to address the hostile crowd. Paul's identification with Tarsus, the great city, distinguished him from the Egyptian assassin and gained the captain's respect. The verse displays the readiness of Paul to bear witness: even bound and beaten, seeking to preach to the mob. The request to speak, daring as it was, displays Paul's zeal for the gospel and his fearlessness before the crowd. The verse sets up the great defense (ch. 22): Paul, having gained the captain's leave, would address the people in Hebrew (v.40). The correction and request display the composure, standing, and boldness of the apostle, turning his arrest into an occasion for testimony.
40:
"And when he had given him licence, Paul stood on the stairs, and beckoned with the hand unto the people. And when there was made a great silence, he spake unto them in the Hebrew tongue, saying," The witnesses note Paul gaining leave and beginning his defense. On the licence, the grammatical voices note the captain's permission. The captain, satisfied Paul was no rebel, allowed him to speak. Calvin notes he would never have let a wicked man address the suspect crowd.

On "beckoned with the hand," the witnesses note the gesture for silence (as 12:17). Paul, standing on the stairs (a vantage above the crowd), waved for attention, and a great silence fell.

On "in the Hebrew tongue," the witnesses note the wisdom of the choice. Barnes: he used the language "understood better by the multitude, and would tend to conciliate them." Chrysostom: "the using the same tongue subdued them... they had a sort of awe for that language." Paul's Hebrew (Aramaic) won their attention.

The thin comment matches the verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: the captain gave leave (satisfied Paul was no rebel, Calvin), and Paul, standing on the stairs, beckoned for silence (as 12:17) and addressed the people in Hebrew (Aramaic) — the language conciliating them (Barnes), subduing them by its familiarity (Chrysostom: they had an awe for it). The verse displays the dramatic scene: Paul, bound, addressing the hushed crowd from the stairs. The verse marks the transition to the great defense (ch. 22): Paul, having gained leave and silence, beginning to speak in Hebrew. The verse displays the providence in the opportunity: the arrest issuing in a public hearing before the very crowd that sought his death. The verse displays Paul's wisdom: using Hebrew to win the crowd's attention and goodwill. The vivid scene — Paul on the stairs, beckoning, the great silence, the Hebrew address — displays the eyewitness narrative and the apostle's composure. The verse displays the turning of the danger into testimony: Paul, bound and beaten, now given a hearing before all Jerusalem. The verse marks the beginning of Paul's defense, the first of the apologies that would carry his witness from the temple stairs to Caesarea, and at last to Rome. The captain's leave, the great silence, and the Hebrew address display God's providence opening a door for the gospel even through Paul's arrest. The chapter closes with the apostle, bound yet undaunted, beginning to bear witness to the crowd that had sought his life.
